title: Geoeconomic Monitor: Latin America Turns Right
gist: A Gavekal teaser flags a rightward political shift across Latin America (Colombia, Peru voting in right-wing leaders this month), with Venezuela's GDP collapse from ~US$370bn (2012) to ~US$100bn cited as a warning against radical leftism—though Brazil's Lula remains favorite for a fourth term in October. A separate note warns Iran's emerging Strait of Hormuz 'protection racket' is a dangerous precedent for global trade.

insights.claim: Latin American electorates are continuing to vote in right-wing leaders, with Colombia and Peru both electing right-wing leaders in June 2026.
insights.speaker: Tom Miller
insights.so_what: A regional rightward shift signals potential pro-market, pro-investment policy turns across LatAm assets and currencies; watch Colombian and Peruvian equities/bonds for re-rating.
insights.quote: electorates continue to vote in right-wing leaders—this month in Colombia and Peru
insights.locator: With tensions cooling in the Middle East

insights.claim: Brazil is expected to buck the regional right-wing wave, with Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva favored to win a fourth term in October's election.
insights.speaker: Tom Miller
insights.so_what: Continued left-leaning leadership in the region's largest economy diverges from the LatAm trend; a Lula win could keep Brazilian fiscal/policy risk premia elevated relative to peers.
insights.quote: the right-wing wave may yet break before it reaches Brazil, where Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva is favorite to win his fourth term in October’s election
insights.locator: But the right-wing wave may yet break

insights.claim: Venezuela's nominal GDP has collapsed to roughly US$100bn today from US$370bn in 2012, a decline cited as a powerful warning against radical leftism.
insights.speaker: Tom Miller
insights.so_what: The ~73% nominal GDP collapse frames the political backlash narrative driving LatAm's rightward shift; a concrete anchor for the anti-leftist thesis.
insights.quote: where nominal GDP has collapsed to about US$100bn today from US$370bn in 2012

themes.theme: Iran and Strait of Hormuz trade risk

insights.claim: Iran's emerging 'protection racket' around the Strait of Hormuz sets a dangerous precedent for global trade, even as Middle East tensions cool.
insights.speaker: Tom Holland
insights.so_what: A normalized shakedown model over a key chokepoint raises structural risk to oil shipping and freight insurance costs; watch energy and tanker rates for embedded geopolitical premia.
insights.quote: Tom Holland explains why Iran’s emerging protection racket sets a dangerous precedent for global trade
insights.locator: Back in the Strait of Hormuz
